other_medical_conditions.explain_other_medical_conditions_line_1: 35-year-old woman with seasonal and environmental allergies (pollen, dust mites, and cat dander) causing sneezing, itchy eyes, and mild wheezing during spring and fall; carries an over-the-counter antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg) and uses it most days during high-pollen seasons. Also has mild intermittent asthma diagnosed in her late teens, mostly exercise-induced or triggered by upper respiratory infections or heavy exposure to pollen; uses an albuterol inhaler as needed, typically 1–2 puffs before running or if she feels chest tightness, averaging use only a couple of times per month outside allergy season.
other_medical_conditions.explain_other_medical_conditions_line_2: History of generalized anxiety and recurrent mild depression, first formally diagnosed in college during a period of academic and financial stress. Currently managed with a combination of weekly talk therapy (cognitive behavioral therapy) and a low-dose SSRI (sertraline 50 mg once daily in the morning). She reports that the medication has significantly reduced episodes of feeling overwhelmed, racing thoughts at night, and difficulty concentrating at work. She still occasionally experiences brief spikes of anxiety in social situations or when work deadlines pile up but has learned several coping strategies such as structured to-do lists, scheduled breaks, and short breathing exercises.
other_medical_conditions.explain_other_medical_conditions_line_3: Reports ongoing but intermittent stomach and digestive issues over the last 5–6 years, primarily consistent with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) rather than ulcers: episodes of cramping, bloating, and alternating loose stools and constipation, often worse during periods of high stress or when she eats a lot of dairy or very greasy foods. She has not had any gastrointestinal bleeding, black or tarry stools, or unexplained weight loss. Symptoms improved somewhat after she started limiting lactose, increasing fiber gradually, and drinking more water. She keeps a symptom journal on her phone, noting that long workdays where she skips meals or eats fast food tend to make things worse. No other significant medical conditions, hospitalizations, or surgeries to date.
